questions.text: I feel a strong pull to be deeply involved in important initiatives, though not necessarily in charge.
I notice that my emotional responses have a ceiling — they rise to a point and naturally level off.
I hold the view that self-determination means consistently choosing what's best for me.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the one reality that should make every reasonable person pause and reconsider their priorities.
I find that subtext is something I recognize in books more easily than in real life.
I keep a journal or log to reflect on my progress and hold myself accountable.
I find that putting my own projects first leads to my best outcomes.
I learn what I want to achieve by starting to act, not by planning in advance.
I naturally step into the lead when a group seems directionless.
I rarely notice when someone is trying to impress a particular person in a group.
I routinely turn down personal opportunities if they would take me away from people who rely on me.
I find that emotional steadiness during others' pain is a quality I regard as one of my most useful characteristics.
I find that I'm living life with the amplifier turned to ten.
I hold the view that the person who adapts the fastest is the one who succeeds.
I feel that gathering broad input helps reassure the people around me.
I notice that my mood doesn't fluctuate much throughout the day.
I detect the emotional residue in a room where something intense just happened.
I believe that the ability to evaluate human pain without emotional entanglement is what makes certain professions possible.
